title: auto_learn::detect_preferences uses string-prefix heuristics that miss obvious cases
description:
## Severity
Minor
## Dimension
kiss
## Location
src/auto_learn.rs:275-315 (detect_preferences)
## Violation
```rust
let preference_patterns = [
    ("always ", "style"),
    ("never ", "style"),
    ("prefer ", "style"),
    ("don't use ", "style"),
    ("use ", "workflow"),
];
for (prefix, category) in &preference_patterns {
    if trimmed.starts_with(prefix) && trimmed.len() < 200 { ... }
}
```

This recognizes "always use tabs" but not "Always use tabs" (case-sensitive after `to_lowercase`), and not the equally-natural "I always..." or "We should always...". The 200-byte cutoff arbitrarily refuses real preference statements that include reasoning ("always use snake_case because the team has agreed in the style guide..."), pushing useful corrections out of the auto-learner while still capturing terse one-liners that may be sarcasm or quoted text.

The "correction" pass has the same problem: "Actually," (capital A) does not match because the input is lowercased before comparison, but `correction_starts` are also lowercase — so that case actually works. But "stop doing that" matches the literal "stop", catching commands like "stop the server" as user preferences.

`save_learned_preference(category, message.trim(), Some("user_message"))` stores `message.trim()` — but `lower` was used for matching, so the SAVED preference is the original-case text. If the user wrote "ALWAYS USE TABS" the stored value matches the user's intent but the matching key would not. This is a single-pass-mismatch design.
## Mandated refactor
Replace string-prefix heuristics with a small classifier or, at minimum, structured slash-command syntax (`/pref always tabs`). If keeping the heuristic, lowercase BOTH the trim+match input AND store only normalized text; require the message to be ≥ 4 words to filter out command-mode utterances; add explicit denylist for words that change a "preference" into a command (`server`, `service`, `daemon`, ...). Or — most honestly — gate `detect_preferences` behind an explicit user opt-in flag (`auto_learn.preferences = true`) and document that it's heuristic.
## Filed from QA review dispatch
